coredata.dc:title: Superhydrophobic photochromic rewritable paper based on graphene quantum dots for information storage and anticounterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Wan, Jinming
Xu, Jun
Zhu, Shiyun
Li, Jun
Chen, Kefu
coredata.dc:description: As an information carrier, printing paper possesses a wide range of applications in many fields. However, its excessive waste, especially in the field of temporary reading, poses a threat to environmental resources and sustainable development. To alleviate this problem, photochromic materials that can be used for rewritable paper have aroused the interest of researchers. In this study, graphene quantum dots were creatively introduced into organic/inorganic hybrid system, promoting charge transfer induced by UV light and achieving rapid response characteristics. This novel material can achieve a maximum color contrast of 61.92% within 90 s and 86.8% of the maximum value in 10 s. In order to effectively avoid the failure of photoresponsive materials in contact with water, further spraying treatment endowed the material with superhydrophobicity and photoluminescence, forming a safe and stable system of information storage and transmission. Overall, this intelligent responsive material has broad application prospects in the field of anticounterfeiting rewritable paper.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Photochromic
Photoluminescence
Superhydrophobic
Rewritable paper

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for the support of Guangdong Key R&D Program ( 2022B111108004 ); Shandong Jining Key R&D Program ( 2022JBZP003 ); Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Plant Resources Biorefinery ( 2021GDKLPRB-K02 ); National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32200296 ); Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou ( 2023A04J1368 ); Taishan Industiral Experts Programme ( tscx202211068 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of preparation process for functional materials.

(a, b) ATR and Raman spectra of FM-0 and FM-1. (c) UV‐Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of FM-0 and FM-1 after exposure to UV light for 10 s. (d, e, f) SEM images of paper, FM-1 and FM-2 surfaces, respectively. (g, h, i) 3D optical profiles of paper, FM-1 and FM-2 surfaces, respectively.

(a, b, c) ATR-FTIR, Raman and absorbance spectra of FM-1 and FM-1-UV; (d) XRD spectrum of FM-1;(e, f) High-resolution XPS spectra for W4f of FM-1 and FM-1-UV; (g) Photochromic mechanism of the material.

(a) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of FM-1 under continuous UV light irradiation. (b) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of FM-1-UV during self-erasing at room temperature. (c) The CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram of FM-1 and FM-1-UV. (d) L*a*b* values of FM-1 and FM-1-UV. (e) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance of FM-1 at 670 nm during the continuous coloring-erasing process. (f) Optical photos of FM-1 colored under UV irradiation with different doses. (g) Schematic diagram of optical printing of sample. (h) Optical photos in the process of optical printing-erasure of FM-1.

(a) CA data of samples. (b) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of FM-2 and FM-2-UV. (c) Emission spectra of FM-2 under λex = 365 nm. (d) Pictures about surface anti-liquid adhesion process of FM-2. (e) Images of FM-1 and FM-2 after UV light printing. (f) Photos of FM-1and FM-2 after wiping water droplets. (g) Photoluminescence optical photo of FM-1 and FM-2 under 365 nm UV light.
(a, b, c) ATR-FTIR, TG, XPS curve of FM-1 and FM-2. (d, e) Element distribution and element content diagrams of FM-1 and FM-2.

head.ce:title: Superhydrophobic photochromic rewritable paper based on graphene quantum dots for information storage and anticounterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, Plant Fiber Materials Science Research Center, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, Plant Fiber Materials Science Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, Plant Fiber Materials Science Research Center, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Plant Resources Biorefinery, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Plant Resources Biorefinery

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Plant Resources Biorefinery, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qingyuan Huayuan Institute of Science and Technology Collaborative Innovation Co., Ltd., Qingyuan 511500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qingyuan Huayuan Institute of Science and Technology Collaborative Innovation Co., Ltd.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Qingyuan Huayuan Institute of Science and Technology Collaborative Innovation Co., Ltd., Qingyuan, 511500, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, Plant Fiber Materials Science Research Center, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, Plant Fiber Materials Science Research Center

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an information carrier, printing paper possesses a wide range of applications in many fields. However, its excessive waste, especially in the field of temporary reading, poses a threat to environmental resources and sustainable development. To alleviate this problem, photochromic materials that can be used for rewritable paper have aroused the interest of researchers. In this study, graphene quantum dots were creatively introduced into organic/inorganic hybrid system, promoting charge transfer induced by UV light and achieving rapid response characteristics. This novel material can achieve a maximum color contrast of 61.92% within 90 s and 86.8% of the maximum value in 10 s. In order to effectively avoid the failure of photoresponsive materials in contact with water, further spraying treatment endowed the material with superhydrophobicity and photoluminescence, forming a safe and stable system of information storage and transmission. Overall, this intelligent responsive material has broad application prospects in the field of anticounterfeiting rewritable paper.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past two thousand years, paper has played a key role in the continuation and dissemination of civilization [1]. Based on the natures, including abundant original resources, compatibility with handling and processing, recyclability, low cost and so forth, paper is still active in the market of information products. Today conforming to the information society, digital and electronic products have developed vigorously, which promote or force the intellectualization and multi-function of paper materials to a certain extent [2]. Notably, solving the problem of difficult reuse is a key point, owing to its strong relation of demand for raw materials and recovery of waste products. The consequences of failing to solve this problem are massive deforestation, environmental pollution, waste of resources and high costs [3–5].

Considering urgency of controlling energy consumption and related chemical emissions, reuse technology of traditional paper products is still limited. According to a survey, paper fibers can only be recycled 2.4 times on average, and more chemical additives are needed with the increase of recycling times [6,7]. In such case, researchers have been working on rewritable technology for used paper recycling. This technology can be achieved via responsive materials that can produce reversible color changes under certain conditions, such as photochromic [8–11], electrochromic [12–14], thermochromic [15–18] and other materials [19–21]. Owing to precise remote non-contact control of photochromic materials, they have attractive potential in the application of rewritable materials [22,23]. Another point is the encryption of information carrier paper [24]. Paper documents have unique advantages over digital and electronic products in the field of information confidentiality. Its offline and face-to-face interaction still meets most demands of information security in contemporary society. However, its easy readability may lead to leakage of information, which is a major problem [25–27]. Currently, fluorescent materials are one of the most popular approaches for information security, where the information or characteristics are only visible or recognizable under UV light [28–30].

In last few decades, photochromic and fluorescent materials have encountered a suitable period of development opportunities, and made some achievements in the market [31,32]. However, there are still some problems waiting for figuring out. Sun et al. prepared a multifunctional phosphor by doping Bi into LiNbO3 (LNO): Pr3+. This phosphor possessed a fast light response rate, but its maximum contrast was only 34%, which was not conducive to the clear display of information [33]. By combining commercial black leuco dyes with new complementary acid chromotropic dyes, Du et al. prepared black rewritable paper with high purity and high contrast [34]. This rewritable material only lasted for 3 h under indoor light, which could not meet the demands of daily application. Li et al. prepared a fast response dual-mode color switching system using polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and hexatungstic acid as the main raw materials [35]. In this experiment, another PVP layer was constructed on the surface of color change layer to face water-induced fading. The viscous surface after water dissolution was extremely unfavorable for the subsequent application due to its well-known water absorption. Therefore, current researches are focused on increasing color contrast, delaying fading time, and improving waterproof performance on the basis of maintaining fast light response. Considering the lack of application researches on a combination between photochromism and fluorescence, a simultaneous exploration of this open field may unlock more potential of rewritable anticounterfeiting materials.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a rising force of carbon materials, have attracted extensive research interest. Compared with other members of the carbon family (e.g., carbon nanotubes, fullerene, and grapheme), they exhibit stronger quantum confinement effect and edge effect. Non-zero bandgap from the quantum confinement makes it behave in a similar way of semiconductor, and a new work exhibits a deviation from the traditional Dirac Fermion model but agrees well with theory accounted for electron-electron and electron-hole interactions. This may lay a foundation of assisting in electron-hole separation of charge transfer complex. In addition, its large edge effect can accommodate more active sites (e.g., edges, functional groups, and dopants) and excellent dispersibility, which is favorable to an introduction for reaction system by a simple method. These properties, along with chemical stability, biocompatibility, and high surface grafting, enable hoping application in the fields of solar cells [36], photodetectors [37], light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [38], rechargeable batteries [39], supercapacitor [40], etc.

Here, we report a responsive system of organic-inorganic hybrid doped with GQDs and hydrophobic fluorescent mixture. Benefiting by edge effect and dispersibility, GQDs can easily form interactions with reaction system, acting as a bridge between donor and acceptor. On this basis, quantum confinement makes it a bidirectional channel facilitating the separation of electron-holes. This elaborately designed system achieves an improvement of charge transfer, of which its UV-driven color change is reduced to a few seconds and reaches the maximum color intensity within 90 s. An amazing reflectance contrast of 61.92% endows this composite with a visual change of color. With such high contrast, its spontaneous erasure can be completed within 11 h, greatly shortening the reuse cycle. As for the hydrophobic fluorescent layer, nano silica (SiO2) and organic phosphor powders are encapsulated by hydrophobic binder, anchoring to photochromic layer. This functional surface possesses excellent hydrophobicity and resistance to liquid adhesion. Under the irradiation of 365 nm, it can emit clear yellow fluorescence. Based on an ingenious combination of photochromism and fluorescence, we provide a novel strategy for recycling and anticounterfeiting of paper.

In this experiment, ammonium metatungstate (AMT; 99.5%) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP; Mw = 130,000) were collected from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS; SYLGARD® 184 elastomer) was purchased from Dow Corning Corporation. Ethylene glycol (EG) and N-hexane were obtained from Shanghai Runjie Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Phosphor powder (YG-HL1) was collected from Shenzhen Qianbianse Technology Co., Ltd. GQDs (TF-11011, 15 nm) was purchased from Suzhou Tanfeng Graphene Technology Co., Ltd. Nano-silica (SiO2; AEROSIL R974) was collected from Degussa. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2; 30%) was purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory. All reagents were used without further treatment unless otherwise specified.

The preparation of this functional paper was mainly involved in coating and spraying methods (Fig. 1 ). Firstly, a precursor solution of photochromic layer was obtained, by which 750 μL of GQDs solution (1 mg/mL), 5 g of PVP, 50 g of AMT and 1 mL of EG were fully stirring in 21 mL of pure water. Then, this solution was evenly coated on a fiber paper using a scraper (coating thickness was 100 μm). After drying in an oven of 40 °C, the as-dried paper was obtained (FM-1). As a comparison, the material obtained by coating the mixed solution without GQDs was recorded as FM-0. Next, fluorescent powders (0.2 g), nano-SiO2 (0.6 g) and 1.2 g of PDMS (including 0.12 g curing agent) were added into 30 mL of n-hexane for full stirring. Lastly, this solution was sprayed on FM-1 with a spray gun, and the paper was put into an oven for curing (40 °C, 6 h). The as-fabricated material was recorded as FM-2.

Contact angle (CA) of the surface was recorded on a contact angle meter (ZJ-7000, Zhijia, China) with a 5 μL of water drop. Micro-graphs of the surface were investigated by a field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Merlin, Zeiss, Germany) and a 3D optical profilometer (RTEC UP Dual Model, Rtec, USA). Element distribution on sample surface was observed by an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, X-Max Oxford, UK). Chemical composition analysis was measured with a Fourier transform attenuated total reflection infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR; Nicolet IS50 - Nicolet Continuum, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Raman spectra were performed on a laser Raman spectrometer (LabRAM Aramis, Horiba Jobin Yvon, France). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Axis Supra+, Kratos, UK) was applied to obtain the XPS spectra of samples. Thermal stability was measured by a thermogravimetric analyzer (TG209F1, Netzsch, Germany). The reflectance spectra for samples were obtained on an ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was taken by an X-ray diffraction system (X'Pert3, PANalytical B. V, Netherlands). The L*a*b* values were measured on a spectrophotometer (YS3020, 3NH, China). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were characterized with a FluoroMax-4 (Horiba, Japan) fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fluorescence characteristics were observed by UV analyzer (365 nm, WFH-203B, Shanghai Yixin Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., China). In addition, the distance between UV light (395 nm, 200 W, Shenzhen Zhonglian Jianchao Technology Co., Ltd., China) and an irradiated surface was 5 cm.

As shown in Fig. 2a, characteristic peaks belonging to AMT are located at 735 cm−1, 866 cm−1, and 930 cm−1, which are related to W-Oc-W, W-Ob-W and WO. The peak at 1640 cm−1 is assigned to CO of PVP [41–44]. It can be clearly seen that an addition of GQDs leads to some displacement of these characteristic peaks, but no new peaks. This phenomenon can be interpreted that they only change environment of this complex and form some non-chemical bonds, such as hydrogen bonds or intermolecular forces, but no chemical reactions occur, reflecting their chemical inertness. This conclusion is well supported by the results of Raman spectroscopy, because these spectra behave in a similar way (Fig. 2b). Although the formation of chemical bonds between this hybrid and GQDs is not observed, GQDs can assist in the migration of electrons for this hybrid excited by UV irradiation, due to their doped energy level. From a macro perspective, GQDs promote the photochromic rate of this whole hybrid system, of which FM-1 exhibits a lower reflectance after a 10 s UV irradiation (Fig. 2c).

Besides, surface morphology is related to photochromic characteristics. Base paper is mainly composed of interwoven fibers and fillers in pores (Fig. 2d). After coating and drying treatment, a thin film of 100 nm was formed on a base paper (Fig. 2e). It is worth mentioning that there are some small cracks on the functional layer, increasing specific surface area of irradiation receiver and strengthening its photochromic rate [45]. As is known, rough microstructures play a key role in a formation process of hydrophobic layer. However, SEM images clearly exhibit that this photochromic coating possesses a flat surface, and surface roughness diagram shows a low surface roughness of 0.39 μm (Fig. S2a). In views of the hydrophilicity for photochromic layer, PDMS/n-hexane dispersion containing nano-silica (Fig. S1a) and micron phosphor (Fig. S1d) was sprayed on the surface of photochromic layer (Fig. S1b, S1c). As shown in Fig. 2f, abundant rough microstructures are formed with rod-like and spheroidal powders. Quantitatively, a hydrophobic layer is about 29 times rougher than a photochromic layer, which lays a foundation for the subsequent hydrophobic experiment. Fig. 2 g-2i show the three-dimensional structure of the sample surface more vividly, which corresponds to the surface roughness diagram.

Actually, macroscopic behaviors of discoloration for this material are related to the microscopic changes of electron transfer and lattice distortion. ATR-FTIR, Raman and UV–vis diffuse reflectance tests were carried out to capture electron transfer path and structure changes. As is presented in Fig. 3a and Fig. 3b, a downward trend of characteristic peaks belonging to AMT and PVP, and -OH peaks of water molecules occur, showing some deformations of the structure [46,47]. This variation may be corresponded to charge transfer existing in the valence layer of this hybrid, which is located at ∼680 nm (Fig. 3c). As for another characteristic absorption peak at ∼490 nm, it belongs to the d-d transition of non-valence layer in metal, whose energy is not affected by the degree of anion reduction [48]. Along this way, heteropoly blue may be generated by charge transfer between heteropoly acid molecule and organic polymer. In Fig. 3d, this hybrid system exhibits halo peaks without other obvious peaks. There is a major halo peak near 17°, which is the PVP characteristic peak. In fact, AMT and GQDs lay in the amorphous matrix of PVP, and possess weaker signal strength. This may be interpreted by that these inorganic particles were fully dispersed in the solution of PVP with high molecular weight (Mw = 130,000), avoiding their ordered self-assembling in the liquid environment and aggregating during the water evaporation. XPS spectra were obtained for studying electron behaviors of materials in this photochromic process (Fig. S2b). Generally, the valence state of tungsten in AMT is positive hexavalent, but tungsten with positive hexavalent and positive pentavalent was detected in a composite without UV irradiation (Fig. 3e). This abnormal phenomenon may be interpreted by that X-ray irradiates on this hybrid, driving high valence state to low valence state. Similarly, the area ratio of bivalent states further increased from 0.07 to 0.23 after UV irradiation, indicating that more W5+ is transitioned to W6+ (Fig. 3f). Under UV irradiation, the photogenerated electrons transition from the terminal oxygen to the dd orbital of the metal, and the protons from the organic polymer are transferred to the co-side oxygen atoms of the WO6 octahedral lattice, forming a charge transfer complex. In such case, W6+ is reduced to W5+, generating heteropoly blue, and realizing the color change [49]. In this process, GQDs will further improve the charge transfer efficiency and the photochromic rate of this system. The discolored sample will also be oxidized in the air (i.e. W5+ will be oxidized to W6+), thus realizing the spontaneous fading process from blue to white (Fig. 3 g).

Benefitting from the light response, color contrast and spontaneous fading, photochromic paper can be employed to construct a promising rewritable material. In most cases, the photochromic paper reveals a rapid optical response, but its application potential in high contrast and short recovery cycle is unlocked. As a proof of this applicability, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra under UV light were tested. Fig. 4a shows the whole discoloration process of 90 s for this material, of which its reflectance at 670 nm dramatically decreases from 62.59% to 0.67%. It demonstrates a higher contrast than that of many previous reported advances. In a 10 s irradiation, this paper can reach a high contrast of 86.83% for the maximum coloration intensity, which shows its ultra-high photosensitivity. Compared with the rapid photoelectron-driven reaction, the spontaneous oxidation reaction lasts a much longer time, lengthening the whole cycle. Generally, polymers with weak intramolecular interactions are more conducive to gas diffusion. It is similar that oxygen can process a comparatively easy entrance into the photochromic layer, owing to crowded but not tight solid polymer matrix of PVP. This structure expands the reaction area and allows the spontaneous fading time to be completed within 11 h (Fig. 4b). Compared with the long or short self-fading time in previous papers, this material provides temporary reading with appropriate time of stable storage and transmission [50,51]. Additionally, the quick erasure pattern was explored that H2O2 can significantly reduce the fading time of this material no more than 50 min, which can improve its cycle efficiency (Fig. S3).

CIE 1931 XYZ color space (also known as the CIE 1931 color space) is one of the first color spaces defined mathematically to study color perception. In this work, XYZ values were tested to determine the coordinate position before and after color change. As shown in Fig. 4c, original state and colored state of a sample are located in the white and blue areas respectively, displaying a visual color contrast. Furthermore, the photochromic properties of sample are measured by L*a*b* values. It should be noted that value L*represents the brightness (i.e. 0 represents black and 100 represents white); the positive and negative of value a* represent the red and green, and the positive and negative of value b* represent the yellow and blue of an object. From Fig. 4d, it can be seen that color change leads to a significant decrease of value L* from 88.09 to 29.83, a slight increase of value a* from - 2.38 to 0.78, and a chrominance change of b* value from 1.09 to - 4.80. According to the above results, its colored state can be accurately regarded as dark blue. As a kind of rewritable paper aimed at reducing paper waste, its stable circulation characteristics are crucial. From Fig. 4e, the reflectance value of FM-1 is basically stable during 20 cycles of coloring-self fading. Fig. 4f shows the optical photos of the samples colored at different UV doses. It can be found that discoloration causes a significant decrease of L* value, demonstrating massive generation of dark pentavalent tungsten. In addition, the irradiation drives this material to red and blue (value a* changes from negative to positive, and value b* changes from positive to negative). As a kind of rewritable paper aimed at efficient usage of information, and reduction of resource-waste, environment-destroy and energy-consumption caused by papermaking industry, its stable circulation characteristics are important. Based on reversible conversion of colorless hexavalent tungsten to black pentavalent tungsten, this material possesses cyclic properties. Although the lattice distortion caused by revisable reaction may lead to the decline of reaction degree, its color stability is still roughly guaranteed after 20 times of coloring-fading process (Fig. 4e).

For obviously exhibiting the discoloring process, optical photos of samples colored at different UV doses were obtained (Fig. 4f). As the illumination accumulates, more electrons are transferred to the d orbital with hexavalent tungsten converting to pentavalent tungsten, realizing the color deepening. Owing to sensitivity of this photoinduced reaction, a slight UV dose can achieve a significant color change, which provides a certain basis for its application in UV indicator (Fig. S4). Optical printing focuses on information of accurate writing and reading. As aforementioned, this reaction possesses optical sensitivity and deep discoloring degree. These characteristics are directly exhibited in Fig. 4g and Fig. 4h. Optical printing photos of FM-1 has clear display and high resolution. It can still achieve perfect reproduction of the patterns after fading, showing its great potential as an excellent rewritable material.

There is a key problem that has not been emphasized in the introduction, that is, most organic-inorganic photochromic hybrids are troubled by water attack. It also needs special attention owing to a probabilistic material failure of this material after contact with water. In order to change the water sensitivity, we applied to hydrophobic treatment to the photochromic paper. As shown in Fig. 5a, waterproof layer provides excellent hydrophobicity for this paper, increasing its water CA from 99.9° to 153.1°. This phenomenon can be interpreted that micro-nano powders are involved in a formation of rough microstructures and PDMS reduces the surface energy. Specifically, air is trapped in the gaps of small protrusions, weakening its interactions with solid surface. Additionally, these powders are encapsulated by PDMS, of which this hydrophobic binder with low surface energy prevents a water droplet spreading on its surface. This can be confirmed by the results of water anti-adhesion experiment in Fig. 5d. A droplet on the needle can still be separated perfectly after friction with FM-2 surface, and no liquid is attached to the material surface.

By simulating working conditions of contact with water in the actual application, we verify the necessity of a hydrophobic layer for this material. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 5f that dried FM-1 loses its ability to change color while FM-2 is completely unaffected. As is known, a top layer can reduce clarity and contrast of patterns or texts on the bottom layer. However, it still retains most of the readability with a contrast of 56.22% in this experiment (Fig. 5b), owing to high transparency of the hydrophobic layer. For visualizing this difference, optical images were obtained (Fig. 5e). It is hard for naked eyes to tell differences between FM-1 and FM-2. When subjected to UV irradiation of 365 nm, this sample can emit a yellow-green light with a peak of 536 nm due to an existence of phosphor powders in the second functional layer. (Fig. 5c) As for the peak at 443 nm, it originates from fluorescent brightener used for paper whitening in papermaking process (Fig. S5). Actually, this fluorescence allows photochromic materials to work for a new strategy of information protection. For example, confidential or real information on this paper can be read with ultraviolet lamp in a dark condition (Fig. 5g). In such case, this combined application may link readability with authenticity, implementing efficient encryption based on this underlying logic.

Organic fluorescent materials are widely used in frontier fields such as fluorescent probes, information display, biological imaging and organic light emitting diodes (OLEDs), due to their diverse structural variability, good biocompatibility and excellent luminous properties. In this paper, they were sprayed on the photochromic layer for waterproof performance and fluorescent properties. As shown in Fig. 6a, the characteristic peaks belonging to AMT and PVP from FM-2 have disappeared, indicating that coating treatment has a certain coverage effect and reduces the detectability. Additionally, new characteristic peaks correspond to raw materials used in this spray treatment, such as anti-symmetric tensile vibration peak of Si-O-Si at 1095 cm−1, which comes from nano-SiO2 [52]. TG and DTG curves of samples are shown in Fig. 6b and Fig. S6. It can be found that these composites were structurally damaged at approximately 350 °C. This stage is involved in losses of crystal water from cellulose, most degradation of PVP, complete decomposition of inorganic substances introduced by coating and spraying, and initial decomposition of AMT [53,54]. This phenomenon indicates that these materials maintain certain thermal stability. XPS was used to observe element changes of composites during processing to confirm the payload of functional components (Fig. 6c). Compared with FM-1, the surface of FM-2 is mainly occupied with C, O and Si instead of C, O and W. For clearly exhibiting distributions of these elements, EDS was carried out (Fig. 6d and Fig. 6e). Element W plays an important role in a photochromic process. Fast reaction and deep coloring are ensured with a high surface content of 71.58% for W. Due to partial coverage of further spraying treatment, its detectability is greatly reduced with a low surface content of 12.04%. Meanwhile, Si ascends to one of the most abundant elements with a surface proportion of 32.27%, owing to its leading content of a hydrophobic layer. Wherein, PDMS possesses a helical straight chain structure composed of main chain (-Si-O-Si-) and organic group (-CH3), whose hydrophobic groups are outward, resulting in low surface tension. Nano-SiO2 forms rough microstructures with fluorescent powders, cooperating with PDMS to produce hydrophobic effects. These results and analysis are in good agreement with experimental phenomena.

In this work, a novel rewritable paper with excellent anticounterfeiting performance has been prepared, providing a new paradigm for secure and stable information storage and transmission. GQDs are innovatively introduced into an organic/inorganic hybrid system for an improved optical response. This composite quickly responds within a few seconds of UV irradiation and reaches saturation state within 90 s with a maximum coloring contrast of 61.92%. Repeating the coloring-fading process 20 times only results in a slight decrease of its coloring contrast, which is crucial for rewritable material. Its color information can maintain about 11 h at room temperature, providing necessary time for information transmission. In addition, the H2O2 atmosphere is favorable to reduce its fading time no more than 50 min, achieving an increased cycle efficiency in practical applications. The subsequent spraying treatment endows the material with superhydrophobic and photoluminescent properties. Additionally, this functional coating hardly affects the readability of photo printing information. Overall, this work combines GQDs with organic-inorganic hybrid in the field of photoresponsive materials for the first time, providing a novel and robust approach for information storage and anticounterfeiting.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

Jinming Wan: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jun Xu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Shiyun Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Jun Li: Methodology, Conceptualization. Kefu Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the support of Guangdong Key R&D Program (2022B111108004); Shandong Jining Key R&D Program (2022JBZP003); Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Plant Resources Biorefinery (2021GDKLPRB-K02); National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32200296); Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou (2023A04J1368); Taishan Industiral Experts Programme (tscx202211068).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: SEM images, mean roughness, XPS spectra, UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra, L*a*b* values, emission spectra, TG and DTG curves of samples. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.susmat.2024.e00866.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Transparent paper: fabrications, properties, and device applications
